title: The mod $p$ derived spherical Hecke algebra: structure and applications
speaker: Niccolò Ronchetti
abstract: Abstract: I will introduce the mod p derived spherical Hecke algebra of a p-adic group, and discuss its structure via a derived version of the Satake homomorphism. Then, I will survey some speculations about its action on the cohomology of arithmetic manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from degree-shifting Hecke symmetries
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates derived Hecke operators by asking why the same Hecke eigensystems appear in different cohomological degrees of arithmetic groups, and explains that torsion coefficients are needed to construct such degree-raising operators.

proof_stages.label: Local Ext-definition of the derived spherical Hecke algebra
proof_stages.summary: After fixing a finite coefficient ring S, a split p-adic reductive group G, and a hyperspecial compact K, the classical spherical Hecke algebra is reinterpreted as an endomorphism algebra and upgraded to the graded Ext-algebra Ext^*_G(c-Ind_K^G 1,c-Ind_K^G 1), which acts on derived K-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Degree-one derived Satake structure theorem
proof_stages.summary: Under largeness and good-position hypotheses, a degree-preserving derived Satake map to the torus is stated, with injectivity in degrees 0 and 1, a known classical degree-zero image, and a sharp degree-one image bound after localization.

proof_stages.label: Concrete function model and torus calculation
proof_stages.summary: The Ext-algebra is described as equivariant, finitely supported functions on pairs of cosets valued in stabilizer cohomology, and in the split torus case this becomes the group algebra of the cocharacter lattice tensored with the cohomology of the maximal compact torus.

proof_stages.label: Construction of the derived Satake map
proof_stages.summary: The Satake map is sketched as a derived version of integration over the unipotent radical, replacing the classical averaging formula by a sum involving restriction and corestriction maps between stabilizer cohomology groups.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic-manifold action and localization at an eigensystem
proof_stages.summary: For an arithmetic manifold Y_K, the mod p^n derived Hecke actions are passed to an inverse-limit p-adic action, then localized at a Hecke eigensystem chi factoring through Satake so that the torus computation identifies the degree-one operators essentially with Hom(T(Z_p),Z_p) after tensoring with Q_p.

proof_stages.label: Galois, Selmer, and motivic comparison map
proof_stages.summary: Assuming an associated ordinary crystalline Galois representation, a chain of maps is built from degree-one torus Hecke operators through local ordinary deformation cohomology, local duality, a global Selmer group, and a motivic regulator, producing a target with a canonical rational structure.

proof_stages.label: Final rationality conjecture and evidence
proof_stages.summary: The final conjecture asserts that, when the automorphic representation is defined over Q, the p-adically constructed derived Hecke action factors through the preceding Galois-motivic map and preserves the rational structure, with evidence from torus cases and related Taylor-Wiles constructions.

examples.name: Classical arithmetic groups such as SL_n(Z)
examples.note: The speaker names SL_2-type and SL_n-type congruence groups to make the motivating cohomology problem concrete, without deriving any new idea from those particular groups.

examples.name: The F = Q_p largeness condition
examples.note: The special case F=Q_p is used only to illustrate that the technical hypothesis on p reduces to p being larger than the Coxeter number.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker merely states that when F=Q_p the largeness hypothesis reduces to p greater than the Coxeter number

examples.name: Anisotropic torus split at p
examples.note: For an anisotropic torus over Q that is split at p, the maps simplify enough that the conjectural factorization can be checked directly, serving as evidence rather than as a proof ingredient.
examples.engagement_reason: the anisotropic torus split at p is cited as a case where the maps collapse and can be checked, but the check is not carried out in the transcript

passes.reason: as a root-free torus case with zero global split rank but split at p, it is a collapsed reductive case at split-rank extremes used as evidence
